e: Bismuth (Bi, atomic number 83) forms hopper crystals as it cools from a melt: its outer edges grow faster than its center, creating the iconic staircase pattern. The rainbow iridescence comes from thin-film interference in the bismuth oxide layer (Bi₂O₃) — the same physics that makes soap bubbles colorful. Bismuth subsalicylate is the active ingredient in Pepto-Bismol, acting as an antacid and anti-diarrheal.
i: Improved explanation with specific physics of the thin-film interference.

e: Bixa orellana (achiote or lipstick tree) produces seeds coated with bixin, a carotenoid pigment used to make annatto dye. Annatto has been used for over 2,000 years in Central and South America for body paint, food coloring, and rituals. Today it is the most widely used natural food dye in the world, coloring butter, cheese, snack foods, and cosmetics. It is listed as E160b in the EU food additive system.
i: Replaced useless placeholder explanation with informative botanical and economic content.

e: An adult body contains approximately 10 billion capillaries, arterioles, venules, arteries, and veins totaling ~100,000 km in length. The capillaries account for over 99% of this length, with diameters of 5–10 micrometers (just wide enough for red blood cells to pass single file). No cell in the body is more than about 50 micrometers from a capillary, ensuring efficient oxygen delivery.
i: Improved explanation with percentage breakdown and cell proximity detail.

e: Blue whales (Balaenoptera musculus) are lunge feeders: they accelerate into krill patches at ~11 km/h with mouths agape, engulfing up to 110 tonnes of water in a single gulp. The ventral pleats expand accordion-like, then the massive tongue (2,700 kg) pushes water out through the baleen plates, trapping krill. Despite their enormous size, blue whales eat ~4 tonnes of krill per day — almost exclusively small crustaceans called Euphausiids.
i: Improved explanation with specific feeding speed and diet composition.

e: The Bohr radius (a₀ ≈ 0.0529 nm or 52.9 pm) represents the most probable electron-nucleus distance in the ground state of hydrogen in Bohr's 1913 atomic model. While modern quantum mechanics replaced fixed orbits with probability clouds, a₀ remains a fundamental atomic length unit. A human hair is ~70,000 nm wide, making the Bohr radius about 1.3 million times smaller — not just 100,000 times.
i: Improved explanation with the exact Bohr radius value and correct size comparison.

e: The bombardier beetle (tribe Brachinini) stores hydroquinone and hydrogen peroxide in separate reservoirs. When threatened, muscular valves open to the reaction chamber, where peroxidase and catalase enzymes catalyze an exothermic reaction, boiling the quinone spray to ~100°C. The beetle fires in rapid pulses (~500 Hz) through a rotating abdominal tip that can aim 270°. The reaction was the first biological example studied as a model for pulse detonation engines.
i: Corrected l2 from 'chemistry_elements' to 'biology_organisms' (this is an animal biology/defense mechanism fact); improved explanation.

e: BoPET (biaxially-oriented polyethylene terephthalate) — brand name Mylar — is stretched in two perpendicular directions during manufacture, aligning polymer chains to dramatically increase tensile strength and tear resistance. When vacuum-metallized with aluminum (~25 nm thick), it reflects up to 97% of infrared radiation. Originally developed for NASA spacecraft thermal control (first used in Echo 1 satellite, 1960), it became ubiquitous in emergency blankets, food packaging, and helium balloons.
i: Improved explanation with satellite first use and Mylar brand name.

e: Boron (B, atomic number 5) is a metalloid with properties intermediate between metals and nonmetals. Boron trioxide (B₂O₃) is added to glass to create borosilicate glass (brand name Pyrex), giving it a low coefficient of thermal expansion and resistance to thermal shock. Boron is also essential in boron neutron capture therapy (cancer treatment), boron carbide armor, and the world's strongest permanent magnet alloys (Nd₂Fe₁₄B).
i: Replaced informal 'Pyrex!' placeholder explanation with comprehensive informative content.

e: The human brain's ~86 billion neurons fire electrochemical impulses consuming about 12–25 watts of metabolic energy — roughly 20% of the body's total energy budget despite being only 2% of body weight. This makes the brain the most metabolically active organ by mass. EEG measures the summed electrical activity of millions of neurons, detecting signals in the microvolt range through scalp electrodes.
i: Improved explanation with the remarkable energy-to-body-weight ratio.

e: Ground brick is a pozzolanic material: it contains aluminosilicates that react with calcium hydroxide (lime) in the presence of water to form calcium silicate hydrates — the same binding compounds in modern cement. This allows the mortar to harden even underwater. Roman opus signinum (brick dust in lime) made cisterns waterproof; later, canal locks and harbor walls built with brick-lime mortar in 18th-century Britain remain intact today.
i: Improved explanation with the specific chemical mechanism and Roman engineering application.

e: The SI second has been defined since 1967 as exactly 9,192,631,770 cycles of the ground-state hyperfine transition of caesium-133. Modern caesium fountain atomic clocks are accurate to ~10⁻¹⁶ — they would neither gain nor lose one second in 300 million years. The caesium-based definition is more stable than any astronomical measurement and is the foundation of Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) used worldwide.
i: Corrected l2 from 'chemistry_elements' to 'physics_mechanics' (defining the SI second is metrology/physics); improved explanation with clock accuracy and UTC connection.

e: Californium-252 (Cf-252) is one of the most expensive substances on Earth at ~$27 million per gram, because it requires intense neutron irradiation in high-flux nuclear reactors to produce. A gram of Cf-252 emits 2.4 × 10¹² neutrons per second, making it an extremely powerful portable neutron source used for: starting nuclear reactors, detecting smuggled explosives and nuclear material in airports, and diagnosing certain cancers via neutron activation analysis.
i: Improved explanation with specific neutron emission rate and applications.

e: Carbon's four valence bonds enable it to form chains, rings, and complex 3D structures with itself and other elements, creating over 10 million known organic compounds — more than all other elements combined. Carbon exists in radically different allotropes: diamond (4 bonds, tetrahedral, hardest natural material), graphite (3 bonds, hexagonal layers, soft lubricant), graphene (single-layer graphite, strongest material), and fullerenes (C₆₀ 'buckyballs').
i: Improved explanation with the count of organic compounds and allotrope comparison.

e: Despite the misleading name, catgut is derived from the intestinal submucosal layer of sheep (primarily), goats, or cattle. The name likely comes from 'kitgut' (kit = small fiddle) or the Germanic 'Katzendarm' being confused with 'cat's gut'. Natural catgut was the standard for surgical sutures until the 1970s (it is bioabsorbable — the body dissolves it), and remains prized by some orchestral string players for its warm tone and responsiveness.
i: Replaced informal one-line explanation with informative content about etymology and surgical/musical uses.

e: 'Catgut' derives either from 'kitgut' (kit = a small violin) or 'cattlegut' — it has never contained feline material. The intestinal serosa of sheep or cattle is cleaned, treated with alkali, and twisted into strands. Natural catgut surgical sutures are absorbed by the body within 10–90 days (chromic catgut takes longer). For violin strings, natural gut transmits vibrations differently than synthetic materials, producing a warmer, more complex tone favored by many baroque and classical musicians.
i: Improved explanation with the biodegradation timeline and acoustic reason musicians prefer it.

e: Cellulose acetate is produced by acetylating plant cellulose (usually wood pulp or cotton) with acetic acid and acetic anhydride. It was one of the first thermoplastics, replacing highly flammable celluloid (nitrocellulose) in the early 20th century. Applications include eyeglass frames, photographic film base (replacing nitrate film), cigarette filters, and textile fibers (acetate rayon). It is semi-biodegradable, unlike most synthetic plastics.
i: Replaced informal placeholder explanation with comprehensive materials science content.

e: Nitrocellulose (cellulose nitrate) film was used from the 1890s but was extremely flammable — it burns at ~150°C and is difficult to extinguish. Cellulose diacetate ('safety film') was introduced in 1909 for amateur use and diacetate/triacetate replaced nitrate in professional cinema from the 1950s. The downside: safety film suffers from 'vinegar syndrome' — acetic acid off-gassing as it degrades, which autocatalyzes further decomposition. Many films from the 1950s–1980s are at risk.
i: Corrected l2 from 'chemistry_elements' to 'materials_engineering' (film base material is materials science); improved explanation with the vinegar syndrome archival problem.

e: Ceramic glazes are formulated from silica (SiO₂), a flux (to lower melting point — feldspars, calcium, or sodium), and metal oxide colorants (copper = green/blue, cobalt = blue, iron = amber/brown, manganese = purple). During kiln firing at 1,000–1,300°C, these fuse into a glassy amorphous layer bonded to the clay. Lead-free glazes became standard in the 20th century after lead glazes were found to leach into food, causing poisoning.
i: Improved explanation with specific colorant oxides and the lead leaching issue.

e: Each champagne bubble nucleates on a microscopic cellulose fiber from dishcloths or napkins (even in apparently clean glasses). As the bubble rises, it scavenges dissolved CO₂ and flavor compounds. At the surface, the thin film ruptures, projecting a jet of flavor-rich liquid upward — this is what contributes to champagne's characteristic aroma intensity when drinking. Flat champagne has no bubbles to carry these aromatic compounds to the nose.
i: Improved explanation with the nucleation mechanism and role in aroma delivery.

e: Chlorine's name derives from the Greek 'chloros' (yellow-green), reflecting its distinctive color as a gas. Discovered by Carl Wilhelm Scheele in 1774 and recognized as an element by Humphry Davy in 1810, chlorine revolutionized public health when John Maguire first chlorinated a water supply in Maidstone, England in 1897. Today, chlorination prevents millions of deaths annually from cholera, typhoid, and dysentery worldwide.
i: Improved explanation with discovery history and first water chlorination detail.

e: A typical cumulus cloud (~1 km³ volume) contains water at ~0.5 g/m³, totaling ~500,000 kg. The droplets (10–20 μm diameter) have a terminal falling velocity of only ~1–2 cm/s, far less than the ~1–5 m/s updrafts that form the cloud. When droplets grow large enough through collision-coalescence to exceed updraft speed (~2 mm diameter for raindrops), they fall as precipitation. Cumulonimbus thunderstorm clouds can contain over 500,000 tonnes of water.
i: Corrected l2 from 'ecology_environment' to 'physics_mechanics' (cloud formation and buoyancy is atmospheric physics); improved explanation with formation and precipitation mechanism.

e: Cobalt (Co, atomic number 27) was first isolated by Georg Brandt in 1735 — the first element to be discovered since antiquity. Its name comes from German 'Kobold' (goblin) because miners blamed mischievous spirits for the toxic arsenic fumes produced when smelting cobalt-containing ores. Today, cobalt is critical in lithium-ion battery cathodes (used in EV batteries), cobalt-chrome superalloys in jet turbine blades, and was historically essential for the brilliant blue of Meissen porcelain.
i: Improved explanation with 'first element since antiquity' fact and specific battery application.

e: Cockroaches breathe through spiracles (tiny openings) in each body segment, delivering oxygen directly to tissues without needing the head. Their open circulatory system has low blood pressure, so decapitation causes clotting rather than exsanguination. The nerve ganglia in the thorax and abdomen can control basic reflexes independently. Death from decapitation comes only from starvation and dehydration (inability to drink) within 1–3 weeks.
i: Improved explanation with the specific cause of death (starvation/dehydration, not blood loss) and survival timeline.

e: Young coconut water (from unripe green coconuts) is naturally sterile inside the nut and has an electrolyte profile — sodium, potassium, chloride, glucose, and a pH of 5.8 — reasonably similar to blood plasma. Field medics in WWII (Pacific theater) and the Vietnam War used it when sterile saline was unavailable. A 2002 study confirmed its use in two Solomon Islands patients. However, it lacks sufficient sodium for adequate IV replacement and is not a true medical substitute for isotonic saline.
i: Improved explanation with specific electrolyte composition and the 2002 study reference.

e: Cold welding occurs in the vacuum of space when two clean, oxide-free metal surfaces contact each other: without the gas or oxide layers present on Earth, metallic atoms on the two surfaces share electrons and form metallic bonds indistinguishable from those within a single piece. The Galileo spacecraft's high-gain antenna (1991) failed to unfurl — possibly due to cold welding during storage. Space engineers must use special lubricants or non-metallic coatings on moving joints.
i: Improved explanation with the Galileo antenna failure example.

e: Most commercial copper powder is produced by gas atomization: a stream of molten copper is broken into droplets by high-pressure gas jets, which solidify into spherical particles (10–150 μm). Alternatively, electrolytic deposition followed by milling produces dendritic particles with high surface area. Copper powder's high conductivity makes it essential in: conductive inks for printed electronics, powder metallurgy for brake pads, EMI shielding, and anti-fouling marine paints that leach copper ions to prevent biofouling.
i: Improved explanation with specific particle morphology and additional applications.

e: Corian (DuPont, 1967) is a solid surface material composed of ~33% acrylic resin (polymethyl methacrylate/PMMA) and ~67% alumina trihydrate (ATH, from bauxite). ATH gives it fire resistance and a smooth, non-porous surface. When fabricated into sheets under 6 mm thick, the PMMA component allows light transmission, creating a translucent glow. Corian can be thermoformed, routed, and seamlessly joined, enabling architects to create complex curved luminous surfaces for counters, walls, and furniture.
i: Improved explanation with specific composition percentages and thermoforming capability.

e: The cornea (anterior transparent layer of the eye) is the only avascular tissue in the human body. It obtains O₂ by direct diffusion from the atmosphere through the tear film (~21% O₂). Blood vessels would scatter light and blur vision, so the cornea relies entirely on diffusion for both oxygen and nutrients from the aqueous humor. Contact lenses that are not gas-permeable reduce O₂ reaching the cornea, potentially inducing neovascularization (blood vessel ingrowth) — a sight-threatening complication.
i: Improved explanation with the neovascularization risk of low-oxygen contact lenses.

e: Corrugated fiberboard consists of a fluted (wavy) medium sheet sandwiched between two flat linerboards, all made from kraft paper. The arch-shaped flutes provide compressive strength far exceeding the sum of individual flat sheets — a principle borrowed from Roman arch architecture. Single-wall corrugated board has ~3 layers; double-wall has 5; triple-wall has 7. Over 95% of products shipped in the US travel in corrugated cardboard boxes.
i: Replaced informal placeholder explanation with informative materials content.

e: The CMB was detected accidentally in 1964 by Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson using a microwave horn antenna at Bell Labs — they initially thought the uniform noise was pigeon droppings and spent weeks cleaning the antenna. It is the afterglow of the Big Bang's 'recombination epoch' (~380,000 years after the BB), when the universe cooled enough for electrons to combine with protons. The CMB confirms the Hot Big Bang model and its tiny temperature fluctuations (1 part in 100,000) seeded galaxy formation.
i: Improved explanation with the pigeon droppings story and its cosmological significance.

e: Cosmine is a complex tissue found in the scales of Devonian lobe-finned fishes (sarcopterygians like Eusthenopteron). Each cosmine scale contains a network of flask-shaped pores opening to the surface through small canals — interpreted as a lateral line-like sensory system embedded within the scale itself. This structure was subsequently lost in most tetrapod lineages. The discovery helped scientists understand the transition from fish to land vertebrates by studying the sensory systems of our fishy ancestors.
i: Improved explanation with specific fish example and evolutionary significance.

e: Cross-linked polyethylene (PEX) differs from regular polyethylene in that covalent cross-links are formed between polymer chains, creating a 3D network instead of linear chains. This dramatically improves heat resistance (XLPE rated to 90°C vs. 60°C for regular PE), chemical resistance, and stress cracking resistance. PEX pipes can withstand freeze-expansion without cracking because the cross-linked network allows elastic deformation. Applications: plumbing, radiant floor heating, and high-voltage cable insulation.
i: Replaced informal placeholder explanation with informative materials content.

e: New Caledonian crows (Corvus moneduloides) fashion tools from pandanus leaves, wire, and sticks, including compound tools (combining two parts to reach a food item). In 2019, researchers found they can even create tools from materials never encountered before, suggesting genuine mental modeling. Different crow populations maintain distinct tool traditions (stick tools vs. hooked leaf tools), and juveniles learn techniques by observing adults — true cultural transmission of technology.
i: Improved explanation with the 2019 mental modeling study and cultural transmission detail.

e: The cuttlebone (internal shell of cuttlefish, genus Sepia) is a porous aragonite (calcium carbonate) structure with hundreds of gas-filled chambers separated by walls only ~0.1 mm thick. The cuttlefish adjusts the gas/liquid ratio in these chambers via osmotic pumping, changing its overall density with precision to achieve neutral buoyancy at any depth. The cuttlebone's structural efficiency (lightweight yet strong) has been studied as a model for engineering lightweight sandwich panels.
i: Improved explanation with the specific material (aragonite) and engineering research application.

e: Cuttlefish achieve perfect color matching despite colorblindness (only one photoreceptor type). The current leading theory: their W-shaped pupils create strong chromatic aberration, and by adjusting focus at different wavelengths, they can detect color without multiple photoreceptors. Additionally, cuttlefish skin contains three independently controlled cell types: chromatophores (pigment), iridophores (structural color), and leucophores (diffuse reflectors), giving them an extraordinarily rich appearance toolkit.
i: Improved explanation with the chromatic aberration color detection mechanism.

e: The Dead Sea (surface elevation −430 m, the lowest land surface on Earth) loses ~1 m of water level per year primarily because Israel, Jordan, and Syria divert ~90% of the Jordan River's flow for irrigation. Evaporation in the extreme heat removes the remaining water, concentrating the salt (33.7% salinity, ~8× the ocean). As the shore retreats, dissolution of underground salt layers is causing thousands of sinkholes to open along the Dead Sea's receding edges — making former beaches dangerous.
i: Improved explanation with specific salinity comparison and sinkhole mechanism.

e: Diamond's combustion (C + O₂ → CO₂) was first demonstrated by Antoine Lavoisier in 1772 using sunlight focused by a lens onto a diamond inside a sealed jar — he showed that diamonds produce only CO₂ upon combustion, proving they are pure carbon (not a distinct material). In air, diamond ignites at ~700–800°C. In pure oxygen, it burns readily at much lower temperatures. The realization that diamond is just carbon was one of the most shocking discoveries in early chemistry.
i: Improved explanation with the historical significance of Lavoisier's discovery.

e: Dan Frost at the Bayerisches Geoinstitut demonstrated diamond synthesis from peanut butter, chocolate, and other carbon-rich organic materials using multi-anvil presses at ~25 GPa and ~2,000°C — simulating conditions 800 km below Earth's surface. The diamonds formed are small (< 1 mm) and contain impurities, but genuine diamond. The experiment proved that the carbon source is irrelevant — what matters is the pressure/temperature conditions. Deep in Earth's mantle, carbon from subducted ocean sediments may naturally form diamonds.
i: Improved explanation with the natural geological analog and typical diamond size.

e: Each human cell's nucleus contains ~3 billion base pairs of DNA distributed across 46 chromosomes totaling ~2 meters in length, coiled around histone proteins and compacted ~10,000-fold to fit within a 6-micrometer nucleus. With ~37 trillion cells, total body DNA length is ~74 trillion meters, or ~74 billion km — enough to reach the Moon ~196,000 times or stretch to the Sun and back ~240 times. All this genetic material fits in cells so small that 100,000 could sit on a pinhead.
i: Improved explanation with the correct Moon and Sun trip calculations.

e: Each cell's DNA suffers ~10,000–100,000 damaging events per day including: oxidative lesions from reactive oxygen species (metabolism), UV-induced thymine dimers (sunlight), spontaneous hydrolysis (depurination), and replication errors. Over a dozen repair pathways exist, with base excision repair (BER) being most common. Cumulative unrepaired damage that affects tumor suppressor genes or oncogenes drives carcinogenesis. The repair machinery is so efficient that most cells go a lifetime without a single mutation being retained.
i: Improved explanation with specific damage types and repair pathway names.

e: Cetaceans breathe voluntarily (unlike humans' automatic respiration), so they cannot fully lose consciousness. Dolphins use unihemispheric slow-wave sleep (USWS): one hemisphere sleeps (~2 hours) while the other stays alert, then they switch. The eye connected to the sleeping hemisphere closes. Humpback whales were recorded 'logging' (motionless at the surface) for ~11% of the day — their mode of rest. Some dolphins spend up to 8 hours per day in USWS but never achieve full bilateral sleep like land mammals.
i: Improved explanation with whale logging behavior and time spent in USWS.

e: CO₂'s triple point (where solid, liquid, and gas coexist in equilibrium) is at 5.18 atm and -56.6°C. At standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm), solid CO₂ sublimates at -78.5°C. To observe liquid CO₂, you need pressurized containers (fire extinguishers contain liquid CO₂ under pressure). The -78.5°C temperature of dry ice makes it useful for preserving biological samples, cold transportation, and stage fog effects (it chills surrounding air causing water vapor to condense into visible fog).
i: Improved explanation with the triple point conditions and fire extinguisher example of liquid CO₂.

e: Duplex stainless steel (typically ~50% austenite + ~50% ferrite) combines the corrosion resistance of austenitic stainless (304/316) with the higher strength and stress corrosion cracking resistance of ferritic grades. Common grades include 2205 (22% Cr, 5% Ni, 3% Mo) and super duplex 2507. They are used in offshore oil platforms, chemical process equipment, desalination plants, and paper mills — anywhere seawater, chloride, or acidic environments require both strength and corrosion resistance.
i: Improved explanation with specific grade compositions and application environments.

e: Earth's inner core (radius ~1,220 km, mostly iron-nickel alloy) is at ~5,400°C ± 600°C — comparable to the Sun's photosphere (5,778 K). This extreme pressure (~360 GPa = 3.6 million atm) raises iron's melting point above the actual temperature, maintaining the solid state. Seismic waves (specifically the absence of S-waves from the outer core and their presence in the inner core) provided the first evidence of the inner core's solid state. The outer liquid iron core generates Earth's magnetic field by dynamo action.
i: Improved explanation with the seismic evidence for solidity and the dynamo mechanism.

e: Earth's rotation is slowing by ~2.3 ms per century due to tidal friction from the Moon. Angular momentum is conserved by transferring from Earth's spin to the Moon's orbit, pushing the Moon ~3.8 cm farther away per year. Tidal rhythmites (layered sedimentary rocks recording ancient tidal cycles) from Western Australia dated to ~1.4 billion years ago show days were about 18.7 hours long. Ancient coral growth rings from 380 million years ago indicate ~22-hour days and ~400 days per year.
i: Improved explanation with Western Australia tidal rhythmites example and coral evidence.

e: When Earth was young and largely molten (~4.5 billion years ago), dense siderophile (iron-loving) metals like gold, platinum, and iridium should have sunk entirely into the iron core, leaving the crust essentially gold-free. Yet Earth's accessible gold is ~20× more abundant than expected. The excess is explained by the 'Late Veneer' — a terminal bombardment of metal-rich meteorites ~3.9 billion years ago that added gold to the solidified crust. This theory is supported by identical tungsten isotope ratios in ancient Greenland rocks and meteorites.
i: Improved explanation with the 'Late Veneer' hypothesis name and tungsten isotope evidence.

e: Earth's magnetosphere (generated by the liquid outer iron core dynamo) deflects the solar wind — a continuous stream of charged particles at 400–800 km/s. Mars's core cooled and solidified ~4 billion years ago, shutting down its dynamo. Without a magnetic field, solar wind sputtering gradually stripped Mars's upper atmosphere, reducing atmospheric pressure from perhaps 1+ bar to the current 0.006 bar. The Mars Atmosphere and Volatile Evolution (MAVEN) spacecraft confirmed solar wind escape rates of ~100 grams/second today.
i: Improved explanation with MAVEN mission data and current escape rate.

e: Earth's magnetic field has reversed 183 times in the last 83 million years, recorded in volcanic rocks (paleomagnetic evidence). The last full reversal (Brunhes-Matuyama boundary) was 780,000 years ago. During a reversal, the field weakens to ~10% of normal and becomes chaotic with multiple poles coexisting for thousands of years. Modern concerns: the South Atlantic Anomaly (a current weakening patch over the South Atlantic) may indicate an upcoming reversal, which could affect satellite electronics and cosmic ray exposure.
i: Improved explanation with the specific reversal count and South Atlantic Anomaly as evidence of possible upcoming reversal.

e: The deep biosphere extends at least 5 km below Earth's surface, with viable microbes found in boreholes and deep mines. These organisms ('subsurface lithoautotrophs') survive without sunlight: they oxidize hydrogen from radiolysis (radiation splitting water molecules) and serpentinization (iron-bearing rocks reacting with water). A 2018 Deep Carbon Observatory study estimated the deep biosphere contains 15–23 billion tonnes of carbon in microbes — comparable to about 385 times all the carbon in humans on Earth's surface.
i: Improved explanation with the 2018 Deep Carbon Observatory findings and carbon mass comparison.

e: Earth is an oblate spheroid: its equatorial radius (~6,378 km) exceeds its polar radius (~6,357 km) by ~21 km per pole, giving an equatorial diameter ~43 km wider. This bulge results from Earth's rotation creating centrifugal effects. The consequence: Mount Chimborazo in Ecuador (6,268 m above sea level) is 6,384 km from Earth's center — 2,168 m farther than Everest (which is taller above sea level but at a higher latitude where the Earth is narrower). Chimborazo, not Everest, is the point farthest from Earth's center.
i: Improved explanation with specific radius values and the Chimborazo vs. Everest comparison.
